coredata.dc:title: Selective fluorescent sensing of α-amanitin in serum using carbon quantum dots-embedded specificity determinant imprinted polymers
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Feng, Liming
Tan, Lei
Li, He
Xu, Zhiguang
Shen, Guixian
Tang, Youwen
coredata.dc:description: α-amanitin could make patients die of acute liver failure within a short time if suitable treatment is not provided in a timely fashion. This paper demonstrates a new strategy for direct detection of α-amanitin in serum using carbon quantum dots-embedded specificity determinant imprinted polymers. According to the structure of α-amanitin, we selected a proper moiety of α-amanitin as specificity determinant to synthesize template to prepare the MIPs. The computer simulation was used to screen out acidic methacrylic acid (MAA) and basic 4-vinyl pyridine (4-Vpy) together as functional monomers, and the experiments further proved that synergistic interaction of MAA and 4-Vpy was beneficial to enhance the recognition capability of MIPs for α-amanitin. Moreover, the fluorescence intensity showed good linear correlations with the concentration of α-amanitin from 0.05 to 4.0μgmL−1. The detection limit for α-amanitin was 15ngmL−1. The nanoparticles were employed to directly detect α-amanitin in serum without any pretreatment with recoveries of 97.8–100.9%.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Specificity determinant
Molecular imprinting
Carbon quantum dots
Α-amanitin

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of MIP coated with CDs (MIP-CDs)
Preparation, optimization and characterization of MIP-CDs
Time response of MIP-CDs composites for fluorescence detection of α-amanitin
Effect of temperature on fluorescence detection of α-amanitin
Effect of pH on fluorescence detection of α-amanitin
Fluorescence sensing of α-amanitin using MIP-CDs composites
Selective detection of α-amanitin in serum

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Illustration of the preparation of MIP-CDs.

Optimized three-dimensional structure of α-amanitin through molecular modeling (a); computer simulation of the interaction of template, MAA and 4-Vpy (b). The hydrogen atoms of (a) and (b) are omitted for clarity.

(A) & (B) TEM images of CDs and MIP-CDs; (C) Photoluminescence emission spectra of the CDs-AA solution (recorded from 320nm to 400nm in 10nm increments and from 400nm to 440nm in 20nm increments); (D) Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs (a), CDs-AA (b), NIP-CDs (c), MIP-CDs after (d) and before (e) template extraction.

Effects of α-amanitin concentration (0.05μgmL−1−4.0μgmL−1) on (A) the fluorescence spectra of the MIP-CDs composites; (B) the fluorescence spectra of the NIP-CDs composites. Insets of (A) and (B) are the α-amanitin concentration calibration curves.
The selectivity factor of MIP- or NIP-CDs to α-amanitin and its analogs.

head.ce:title: Selective fluorescent sensing of α-amanitin in serum using carbon quantum dots-embedded specificity determinant imprinted polymers

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Environment, MOE Key Laboratory of Laser Life Science, South China Normal University, 510006 Guangzhou, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Environment, MOE Key Laboratory of Laser Life Science, South China Normal University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangzhou Center for Disease Control and Prevention, Guangzhou 510440, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangzhou Center for Disease Control and Prevention

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: α-amanitin could make patients die of acute liver failure within a short time if suitable treatment is not provided in a timely fashion. This paper demonstrates a new strategy for direct detection of α-amanitin in serum using carbon quantum dots-embedded specificity determinant imprinted polymers. According to the structure of α-amanitin, we selected a proper moiety of α-amanitin as specificity determinant to synthesize template to prepare the MIPs. The computer simulation was used to screen out acidic methacrylic acid (MAA) and basic 4-vinyl pyridine (4-Vpy) together as functional monomers, and the experiments further proved that synergistic interaction of MAA and 4-Vpy was beneficial to enhance the recognition capability of MIPs for α-amanitin. Moreover, the fluorescence intensity showed good linear correlations with the concentration of α-amanitin from 0.05 to 4.0μgmL−1. The detection limit for α-amanitin was 15ngmL−1. The nanoparticles were employed to directly detect α-amanitin in serum without any pretreatment with recoveries of 97.8–100.9%.

ce:section.ce:para: Several studies on toxicity and lethal actions of mushroom revealed that the majority of those cases are caused by genus Amanita and three associated cyclopeptides, viz. amatoxins, phallotoxins and virtoxins (Christine and Hans, 2003; Gupta et al., 2002). Compared to other two classifications, amatoxins are closely related with serious poisoning and they include nine known compounds, including α-amanitin, which is the most toxic compound having a bicyclic octapeptide structure. It has been found that the most amanitin-containing mushrooms comprises of approximately 40% of amanitin of the total content (Cochet-Meilhac and Chambon, 1974; Kedinger et al., 1970; Lindell et al., 1970), and the lethal dose of α-amanitin is 0.1mgkg−1 (Wieland, 1986). Commonly, patients suffering from α-amanitin poisoning die of acute liver failure if not treated on time (Thiel et al., 2011). Moreover, in order to give the targeted therapy, the effective treatment requires fast and accurate diagnosis, as well as real-time monitoring of the amount of α-amanitin in body fluid of the patients. Thus, specific recognition, rapid and a sensitive detection of α-amanitin in the body fluid play a vital role in the recovery of patients.

Since the metabolism kinetics of α-amanitin has been studied through analyzing the blood of patients (Faulstich, 1979), high phase liquid chromatography (HPLC) (Jaeger et al., 1993), HPLC combined with electroanalysis (Oliver et al., 1996; Defendenti et al., 1998) or mass spectroscopy (Maurer et al., 1997; Hans et al., 2000; Filigenzi et al., 2007) are the principal methods for the detection of α-amanitin. In terms of the above methods, an intricate pretreatment of the samples is essential because of the complex composition of the body fluid. However, such pretreatment requires a number of steps and costs a long time to ensure a complete extraction and precise determination of the targeted compound. Therefore, a direct determination of α-amanitin without any pretreatment steps is highly desirable with respect to fast diagnosis and timely therapy. To the best of our knowledge, such direct detection method has not been reported to date.

Molecular imprinting is a facile and straightforward technique that can create specific three-dimensional sites through some templates to recognize a target molecule and its analogs in complex samples. The generation of such imprinted sites can be achieved through co-polymerization of template molecules and functional monomers in an excess of cross-linkers and proper quantities of an initiator, followed by removal of the template via appropriate physical and chemical methods. The final product is molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs), which can display specific recognition to target molecules and mimic natural receptors (Alizadeh et al., 2010), biomimetic catalysis (Li et al., 2011), and drug releasers (Alvarez-Lorenzo and Concheiro, 2004) in this respect. Additionally, MIPs are simple to use, has good stability, high specificity, reusable and cost-effective (Haupt and Mosbach, 2000; Ge and Turner, 2008; Chen et al., 2011).

Despite tremendous advances made in molecular imprinting of small organic molecules, there are still many obstacles to overcome in developing direct imprinting ways of biomacromolecules because some of them are unobtainable and expensive. Additionally, many challenges of imprinting biomacromolecules result from the formation of stable cavities to couple with unsteady protein structure and the rebinding inefficiency due to restricted mobility of biomacromolecules in highly cross-linked polymer networks (Shiomi et al., 2005; Hoshino et al., 2010). The epitope imprinting is a potential way to solve the above-mentioned problems (Verheyen et al., 2011). The technique is based on the use of a small exposed peptide part of a protein, which is employed to synthesize MIPs to effectively recognize the target biomacromolecule (Zhao et al., 2014). The key to success with this method is choosing and identifying the proper epitope. Inspired by the epitope imprinting technology, our group (Li et al., 2014) was the first to report a new approach, in which a ligand specificity determinant was employed as the template to prepare MIPs as artificial α-amanitin receptors through coordination interaction and hydrogen bonds.

The combination of the specific recognition of MIPs and the excellent optical properties of quantum dots for signal amplification and optical readout have drawn great attention (Tan et al., 2013; Lin et al., 2009). Carbon quantum dots (CDs) as a novel nanomaterial have a lot of advantages, including strongly tunable photoluminescent properties, low cytotoxicity, wonderful bio-compatibility and eco-friendliness (Baker and Baker, 2010). Therefore, they have a wide range of applications, such as in bioimaging (Cao et al., 2007; Yang et al., 2009; Gill et al., 2008), improving medical imaging equipment and tiny light-emitting diodes, but have been rarely employed to fabricate chemical sensors (Goncalves et al., 2010).

In the current research, a direct assay of α-amanitin in serum has been used. On account of the high price and large size of α-amanitin, a template with high specificity toward α-amanitin was used to prepare the MIP acceptor. The functional monomers were screened and the proportion between the template and the monomers was optimized through computer simulation. CDs were used as antennae for selective recognition signal amplification and optical readout, and the MIPs shell allowed only specific adsorption, thereby preventing any interfering species from coming into contact with the CDs. This fluorescence sensor was used for the selective and sensitive detection of α-amanitin in serum without any pretreatment.

All chemicals used were of analytical grade. Citric acid anhydrous, allyl amine (AA), N,N′-methylenebisacrylamine (MBAAM) and ammonium persulfate (APS) were purchased from Tianjin Chemical Reagents Co. Ltd. (Tianjin, China). 4-Vinyl pyridine (4-Vpy), methacrylic acid (MAA), N-acetyl-tryptophan, propylamine, tryptophan, glutathione, glucose and bovine serum albumin (BSA, MW 68kDa, pI 4.9) were obtained from Guangzhou Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (Guangzhou, China). α-Amanitin (minimum 90% purity) was used as received from Sigma-Aldrich Co. (St. Louis, MO). All aqueous solutions were prepared with double deionized water.

High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images were taken on a JEM-2100HR (JEOL, Japan) transmission electron microscope operating at 200kV to characterize the morphology of CDs and MIP-CDs. All Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopic measurements (4000–650cm−1) were carried out on a PE Spectrum One FT-IR Spectrometer (Foster City, CA, USA). The fluorescence spectra were collected on an F-2500 Hitachi spectrometer (Tokyo, Japan). The acidity was measured with a pH 211 microprocessor pH meter (Shanghai, China). Nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectra were recorded on a Bruker Vector33 400MHz spectrometer (Bremen, Germany).

The binding energies (ΔE) between the designed template and the functional monomer were calculated using the Gaussian 03 software package (Fritsh et al., 2004). The molecular geometries of complexes between the template and 4-Vpy, the template and MAA, the template with 4-Vpy and MAA were optimized at B3LYP/6-31G⁎⁎ level (Becke, 1993; Lee et al., 1988). In addition, the stable structures were further confirmed through analytical computation of the harmonic vibrational frequencies. The binding energies (ΔE) of the complex were calculated using the following equation:
Δ E = E
( complex )
- E
( functional
monomer )
- E
( template )
.

The synthetic procedure of the template was similar to an approach described previously in the article (Li et al., 2014). To a 250mL round-bottom flask, 4.0 gN-acetyl-tryptophan and 100mL methanol were added and dissolved via ultra-sonication. To the homogeneous solution, 5mL concentrated sulfuric acid was further added the catalyst and the contents were heated to reflux at 50°C for 3h. On cooling to room temperature, the pH of the mixture was adjusted to 7 by using 1molL− 1 of aqueous K2CO3 before transferring it to a separatory funnel. A saturated NaCl solution was then added to it and the mixture was allowed to stand and layer for 30min. After extracting the water phase by utilizing 120mL CH2Cl2 (3×40mL), the organic oil phase was rinsed with 60mL water and 50mL1molL−1 of aqueous K2CO3. Subsequent evaporation of the organic solvent was performed in vacuo at 30°C to obtain white crystalline solid that would react with the added 50mL of propylamine mixed with 50mL of methanol at 25°C to yield a new product. This system was kept stirring for 2 days before evaporating any excess propylamine in vacuo at 55°C. The concentrated crude product was recrystallized in CHCl3 three times and then dried in vacuum at 40°C. This resulting substance was then characterized by FT-IR and NMR. Infrared spectrum (Fig. S1) of this product yields the following: 1630cm−1 (σ C=O, s), 1554cm−1 (δ N–H, m). 1H NMR (Fig. S2) analysis yields the following: δ 7.71 (1H, d, -NH indole), δ 7.34~7.15 (5 H, m, aromatic protons), δ 4.67 (1 H, dd, J=13.3, 7.9Hz, Hα, –CH2–CH- (–NHCO)(–CONH)), δ 3.27~3.01 (3 H, m, CH3CH2CH2N and Hβ), δ 1.14 (2 H, tt, J=20.7, 10.3Hz, CH2–CH2–CH3), δ 0.65 (3 H, m, CH2–CH2–CH3). All of these data indicated that this product was what we designed and expected to obtain.

The preparation way of CDs was referred to the method reported by Yang et al. (2012), Mao et al. (2012) and Zhang and Chen (2014). As the carbon source, androus citric acid (2.1g, 10mmol) was first heated in the oven at 180°C for 15min to generate a yellow solid, which was further dissolved in 10mL aqueous NaOH (0.5M) through sonication. The pH of the solution was adjusted to 6 by drop wise addition of aqueous NaOH (2M) to obtain the final CDs solution. To remove any interfering small ions and molecules, dialysis of the CDs solution was performed using a dialysis bag (MWCO: 3500Da). The double deionized water of dialysis was changed in every 10min until the pH of it was 7. The purified CDs solution was stored in dark at 4°C. Solid CDs were obtained by rotary evaporation at low pressure and dried in vacuum at room temperature.

The approach for synthesizing the CDs-AA was referred to the method reported by Yin et al. (2013). To a 50mL round-bottomed flask, 20mL of CDs solution, 1mL EDC (0.10M) and 1mL NHS (0.10M) were added and the mixture was stirred for 15min at 40°C for the activation of the carboxyl group. Then, 20μL AA was successively added to the mixture and stirred for 2h in order to be coupled with the CDs via dehydration. Subsequent purification was similar to that of CDs via dialysis in a dialysis bag (MWCO: 3500Da) to remove redundant small ions and molecules. The purified CDs-AA solution was stored in the dark at 4°C and the solid products were acquired through rotary evaporation at low pressure and dried in vacuum at room temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of MIP coated with CDs (MIP-CDs)
ce:section.ce:para: The synthetic protocol of MIP-CDs was carried out under the optimum conditions employing the surface grafting. To a 250mL three-necked flask, 4-Vpy (22μL, 0.2mmol), MAA (18μL, 0.2mmol), and 14.3mg (0.05mmol) template were dissolved completely in 10mL of dimethyl sulfoxide under sonication for 2h. Using a dropping funnel, 10mL of CDs-AA solution was added dropwise (15 drops/min) into the mixture under nitrogen. On complete addition of the CDs-AA solution, MBAAM (150mg), APS (10mg) were further added to the mixture, which was subsequently degassed with nitrogen for 30min. The flask was then sealed and the content was incubated at 45°C in water bath for 24h for polymerization. The control blank CDs modified with non-imprinted polymer (NIP-CDs) was synthesized in the same way without addition of the template. After polymerization, the MIP- and NIP-CDs composites were precipitated by iso-propanol. The precipitate was obtained via centrifugation (15,000r/min) and washed with methanol to remove unreacted monomers and oligomers. The resulting MIP-CDs were collected after Soxhlet extraction by CH3OH/CH3COOH (v/v=7:3) for 48h to remove the template.

Fluorescent measurements were done at an excitation wavelength of 380nm, slit width 5nm (both emission and excitation), and photomultiplier tube voltage 400V. For the array study, 2.0mL MIP- or NIP-CDs solution (0.05M), 1.0mL of phosphate buffer solution (PBS, pH 7.00, 0.10molL−1) and different concentrations of α-amanitin in turn were added to a 4mL calibrated test tube. Subsequently, this mixture was diluted with double deionized water to make it homogeneous.

Serum samples of healthy volunteers were collected from No. 12 Hospital in Guangzhou. The analysis was carried out before 20-fold dilution was made. The recovery study on the purposed method was acted out the spiked samples with different concentrations of α-amanitin by the standard addition method to give the maluation to this developed approach.

The design of the MIP-CDs sensor is based on the fact that the carbon quantum dots (CDs) act as antennae for recognition signal amplification and optical readout, and the molecular imprinted polymers (MIPs) shell provides selective analysis and keeps away interfering molecules from coming into contact with CDs. The preparation steps of MIP-CDs are illustrated in Fig. 1. The CDs (black dot) were first coupled with AA to add the vinyl group to the surface. Then the template (red triangle) and functional monomers were co-polymerized with CDs-AA to generate the MIP-CDs.

As shown in Fig. 2(a), the optimized three-dimensional structure of α-amanitin revealed that the indole ring of tryptophan is situated in a bridge of the bicyclic octapeptides. Based on the consideration that this indole ring and the peptide bonds of the tryptophan N- and C-terminals could undergo π–π interactions and form hydrogen bonds with predetermined functional monomers respectively, we utilized the specificity determinant of α-amanitin to synthesize a new compound as the template to prepare the MIPs.

The prearrangement between the template and the monomer is regarded as a key element for the performance of MIPs, so computer simulation was adopted to screen out MAA and 4-Vpy as functional monomers through the calculation of binding energies between the template and the monomers. It was found that the optimum molar ratio of template, MAA and 4-Vpy was 1:3:1 (Fig. 2(b)). A lower binding energy indicated a stronger interaction between the monomer and the template molecule. The binding energy of hydrogen bonds between template and three MAA molecules was −149kJmol−1, while that of π–π interaction between indole ring of template and pyridine ring of 4-Vpy was −13kJmol−1. The simple sum of the above two binding energies calculated was −162kJmol−1, which is less than the binding energy (−208kJmol−1) of co-interaction of template and two kinds of monomers (Fig. 2(b)). This suggested that the synergistic interaction was generated in the process of prearrangement and could enhance the recognition capability of synthetic MIPs. Significantly, the hydrogen bond between acidic MAA and basic 4-Vpy were probably conducive to a stable configuration of the template-monomer complex and the synergy of hydrogen bonding and π–π accumulation between the template and the monomers.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation, optimization and characterization of MIP-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: In order to synthesize the soluble MIP-CDs by surface imprinting which is different from common sol–gel reaction that involves silane reagents to functionalize the quantum dots (Wang et al., 2011; Mao et al., 2012; Zhang et al., 2012b; Zhou et al., 2014), we designed a protocol (Fig. 1) to prepare them. The crucial procedure of embedding the CDs into the MIPs was implemented by the reaction of terminal vinyl groups on the surface of CDs via the successive adsorption of the monomers and newly formed oligomers in solution during the polymerization. Since MBAAM is soluble in water, it was chosen as the cross-linker to synthesize water-soluble MIP-CDs.

Optimizing the molar ratio of the template and the monomers is an important factor on specific recognition capability of the MIPs (Chen et al., 2011). The optimization experiments were carried out by testing the change of fluorescence intensity of MIP- and NIP-CDs composites after template rebinding. The imprinted factor (IF) was introduced to evaluate the specific recognition capacity of the MIPs to the template, which was calculated by the equation:
IF =
Δ F
MIP - CDs
/ Δ F
NIP - CDs
where ΔFMIP-CDs and ΔFNIP-CDs are the quenching of fluorescence intensity of MIP- and NIP-CDs after rebinding the template, respectively.

As shown in Fig. S3, when MAA or 4-Vpy was used alone to synthesize MIPs/NIPs, the corresponded IF was 1.33 or 1.26. However, when they were used together to prepare MIPs at a molar ratio of 1:4:4, the IF reached up to 3.32, which was larger than the simple sum of 1.33 and 1.26. Thus, the optimization experiments further verified the synergistic effect of MAA and 4-Vpy in the preparation of MIPs, which effectively enhanced the recognition capability of MIPs.

The FT-IR spectrum (Fig. S4) of pure CDs revealed two characteristic peaks at 3600cm−1 and 1700cm−1, which corresponded to the stretching vibration of O–H and C=O. Comparing with the above data, a strong and narrow peak at 1660cm− 1 was assigned to the stretching vibration of C=C, suggesting that the CDs were successfully functionalized by the modification of vinyl group to form CDs-AA. A much larger peak at 3600cm−1 in MIP-CDs than that in CDs-AA indicated that a large amount of O–H were present in MIP-CDs. In addition, another strong characteristic peak of MIP-CDs at 1750cm−1 could be ascribed to the stretching vibration of C=O. These data verified that a large amount of –COOH groups existed in this material. On the other hand, a new peak at 1550cm−1 was assigned to the C=N asymmetric stretching in pyridine ring (Xu et al., 2014). All these bonds further confirm that MAA and 4-Vpy are successfully grafted on the surface of CDs.

As illustrated in Fig. 3A, the morphology of CDs obtained by TEM exhibited spherical in shape and almost uniform in size with an average diameter of 3.5nm, while MIP-CDs composites revealed spherical nanostructure covered by a shell with a diameter of approximately 50nm (Fig. 3B). This is probably due to aggregation of the particles during the preparation of MIP-CDs.

As shown in Fig. 3C, with increasing excitation wavelength in the range 320–440nm, the emission wavelengths of CDs shifted to the longer wavelength from 430nm to 520nm and the fluorescence intensity first increased and then decreased. This result was similar to the work of Das et al. (2014). In addition, the maximum intensity was observed at 460nm emission wavelength, indicating that the corresponding 380nm excitation wavelength was considered to be the best choice for the current study.

The synthesized MIP-CDs were adopted template sensing. As illustrated in Fig. 3D (b) and (e), before template extraction, the fluorescence intensity of MIP-CDs would be quenched approximately to zero. In addition, comparing Fig. 3D (b) and (c), the intensity of NIP-CDs composites was about 38.6% of the intensity of original CDs-AA. In parallel with Fig. 3D, (c), Fig 3 D (d) shows that after template extraction, the fluorescence intensity of MIP-CDs could reach 77.2% of that of the NIP-CDs. These two phenomena of fluorescence decreasing might be ascribed to the process that the fluorescence of CDs passed through a thin MIPs shell which adsorbed a part of the emissive light to a certain extent.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Time response of MIP-CDs composites for fluorescence detection of α-amanitin
ce:section.ce:para: In order to probe the adsorption kinetic of α-amanitin on MIP-CDs, we investigated time response of this newly fabricated sensor. As depicted in Fig. S5, on addition of α-amanitin in the NIP-CDs solution a decrease in fluorescence intensity was observed, which remained constant over time, signifying a lack of specific binding between α-amanitin and NIP-CDs composites. By contrast, the intensity for MIP-CDs composites decreased at a faster rate, reaching a constant value when the concentration of α-amanitin was 0.3μgmL−1 after 20min. The constant intensity value signified an equilibrium state when the adsorption and the desorption rates become equal. This was also chosen as the best response time (20min) for subsequent experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of temperature on fluorescence detection of α-amanitin
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. S6, both MIP-CDs and NIP-CDs have strong fluorescence intensities at 15°C. With temperature increasing from 15 to 50°C, the fluorescence intensities of NIP-CDs decreased from 154 a.u. to 107 a.u., while that of MIP-CDs decreased from 119 a.u. to 55.4 a.u.. In addition, in the range of 15 to 25°C, the fluorescence quenching percentage was relatively steady. In order to obtain stable fluorescence intensity and high sensitivity, 25°C was chosen as the optimum temperature for further experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of pH on fluorescence detection of α-amanitin
ce:section.ce:para: The recognition ability of MIP-CDs at different pH was studied. As can be seen from Fig. S7, the fluorescence intensities of MIP-CDs and NIP-CDs were almost unchanged in the range of 5–9. The change in fluorescence intensity of MIP-CDs was larger than that of NIP-CDs. The maximum quenching percentage of fluorescence intensity was observed at a pH of 7, thus this value was selected for further experiments to obtain stable fluorescence intensity and high sensitivity. The PBS buffer solution was used in typical physiological environments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence sensing of α-amanitin using MIP-CDs composites
ce:section.ce:para:
As presented in Fig. 4A and B, both MIP- and NIP-CDs composites responded to α-amanitin molecule and their fluorescence intensity turned out to be quenched gradually as the α-amanitin concentration increased from 0.05 to 4.0μgmL− 1. Generally, the fluorescence quenching depends upon the adsorptive affinity of the particles with the template (Zhang et al., 2012). The fluorescence quenching in this system followed the Stern–Volmer equation (Chen and Rosenzweig, 2002):
F 0
/ F = 1 +
K sv
[ Cq ]
where F 0 and F are the photoluminescence intensities in the absence and presence of a quencher (in this study, α-amanitin is the quencher), Cq is the concentration of the quencher and K sv is the quenching constant.

As shown in the insets of Fig. 4A and B, both the calibration plots of (F 0/F−1) versus Cq of MIP-CDs and NIP-CDs have a good linear relationship with correlation coefficients of 0.999 and 0.997 over a range of 0.05–4.0μgmL−1. The limit of detection was calculated following the 3σ/S criteria, where σ is the standard deviation of the blank signal and S is the slope of the linear calibration. The detection limit for α-amanitin was 15ngmL−1. The precision for 5 replicate detections of 0.8μgmL−1 was 2.4% (RSD). Here, the ratio of K sv of the MIP- and NIP-CDs was defined as specific factor (SF) and used to evaluate the recognition capability of MIP-CDs for α-amanitin. Based on the slope of these two lines (K sv,MIP-CDs=0.969, K sv,NIP-CDs=0.326), the SF value was 2.97, indicating that the quenching efficiency of fluorescence can be increased and the spectral sensitivity of the MIP-CDs to α-amanitin can be amplified by molecular imprinting technology.

Under optimum conditions, α-amanitin, tryptophan, glutathione, N-acethyltryptophan and BSA (Fig. S8) were selected as the analog target compounds for the template to investigate the selective recognition capacity of MIP-CDs. The selectivity factor (γ) was calculated by the equation:
γ
MIP - CDs
(
γ
NIP - CDs
)
= Δ
F template
/ Δ
F analogue
where ΔF template and ΔF analog are the quenching fluorescence intensity of MIP-CDs or NIP-CDs after rebinding of the analytes, respectively. Obviously, closer the value of γ is to 1.00, higher is the selectivity of MIP-CDs for the template.

Table 1 reveals that the selectivity of MIP-CDs is closely related with the structural similarities between the analogs and the template. Regardless of the size, the structure of α-amanitin corresponds most closely to the template among the analogs. The MIP-CDs for α-amanitin exhibited an excellent selectivity factor of 1.26 among the analogs, which is most proximate to 1.00. The structure of N-acetyl-tryptophan was similar to the template, but it only contains one peptide bond compared to the template. So the γ of MIP-CDs for it was 1.57. Since tryptophan has no peptide bonds like the template, α-amanitin and N-acetyl-tryptophan, the MIP-CDs for it showed γ value 1.72. Therefore, although tryptophan in all selected analogs is most likely to be found in serum, we expect that tryptophan has few interference on recognizing α-amanitin in serum by MIP-CDs. BSA contains many building units that are similar to the template molecule, but due to its large size, these building units are hardly accessible to be bound to the imprinted cavities of random distribution in MIPs. Thus, the selectivity factor of MIP-CDs for BSA tops 4.63. Moreover, the selectivity factors of NIP-CDs for the template and all of analogs approach to 1.00, indicating that NIP-CDs have no selectivity for the analogs and the template.

We further investigated the effects of relevant ions in the blood on the detection of α-amanitin by MIP-CDs. As shown in Table S1, the quenching percentage of fluorescence intensities of all the selected ions were less than ±5%, indicating that these ions coexisted in biological fluids have negligible effects on the detection of α-amanitin.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selective detection of α-amanitin in serum
ce:section.ce:para: The important aim of fabricating the fluorescence sensor is to detect α-amanitin in serum without any pretreatment. Fig. S9 shows good fluorescence signals of MIP-CDs for three serum samples, spiked with different concentrations of α-amanitin, even though the fluorescence background of the complex matrix of serum (Fig. S9 (e)) exists. As shown in Table S2, the recoveries for the serum samples were 97.8–100.9%, which were evaluated by comparing the analytical signals of α-amanitin obtained from the spiked serum with those of the same concentration standard solution (n=3, average of three replicate measurements). This result indicated that this new sensor has great potential for real-monitoring of α-amanitin in blood.

An advanced biomimetic α-amanitin fluorescence sensor at neutral pH can be constructed from the rationally designed molecular imprinted polymers. Different from other detection ways, we have developed a new strategy which combined the specificity determinant imprinted with eco-friendly and low-toxic quantum dots to achieve the goal of direct and fast detection of α-amanitin in serum without any pretreatment. The computer simulation was used to successfully screen functional monomers. The optimization experiments verified the synergy effects of MAA and 4-Vpy in the preparation of the MIPs. Since the fluorescence response of MIP-CDs as α-amanitin sensors has minimal interferences from the other constituents of the serum, the presence of α-amanitin is signaled with high sensitivity and selectivity by emission intensity changes of MIP-CDs. This fluorescence sensor should promise a new efficient modality for fast detection and timely treatment.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No.21275057).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.bios.2015.03.005.

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: Gupta, S.K., Kaleekal T., Peshin, S.S., 2002. Emergency Toxicology : Management of Common Poisons. CRC Press, Florida.
Wieland, T., 1986. Peptides of poisonous Amanita mushrooms. Springer Varlag, New York.
